name: Proto-Mashriqi Arabic
notes: Proto-Mashriqi (Eastern) Arabic — GROUPING NODE (glue node) for Egyptian (ar-EG), Sudanese (ar-SD), Chadian/Nigerian West-Sudanic (ar-TD → ar-NG) and the Levantine subtree (ar-x-levantine → ar-SY/ar-LB/ar-JO/ar-PS), plus Mesopotamian ar-IQ/ar-IQ-x-qeltu. It declares only what the dialectological literature treats as SHARED across the whole Eastern group, and serves as the data-inheritance root the leaves build on; every reflex that splits WITHIN the group (qāf, ǧīm, interdentals) is documented here as leaf territory and is NOT asserted group-wide. GENUINELY GROUP-WIDE, MODELLED HERE: (1) SHORT-VOWEL RETENTION — the defining Eastern trait relative to Maghrebi: the triangular short-vowel system /a i u/ is retained (Watson 2002:9: the western group shows 'the typical reduction of the triangular system of short vowels, a, i, u ... to a two-vowel system' while the East keeps it), and the Maghrebi combination of iambic stress plus deletion of unstressed vowels yielding word-initial clusters is 'not typically attested in eastern dialects' — Watson 2002:9 contrasts Moroccan smin with Cairene simīn 'fat' and Lmnabha bṛṛa with Cairene baṛṛa 'outside'. Modelled by NOT giving the short vowels deletion candidates (contrast ar-x-maghrebi, whose a/i/u each license Ø). (2) TROCHAIC WORD STRESS — Watson 2002:9: 'a contrast between an iambic word-stress system in the western group and a trochaic word-stress system in the eastern group', katab stressed 'katab in the East vs ka'tab in the West; the quantity-sensitive stress block below implements the Eastern (trochaic-type) cascade. Watson's own fn.9 caveat: a few eastern dialects (Negev Bedouin, Upper Egypt, Oman) attest iambic stress — a leaf may override. (3) LOSS OF THE CLASSICAL LATERAL ḌĀD: ض is the pharyngealized stop /dˤ/ everywhere in the group (the lateral fricative ɮˤ of Sibawayh's description survives only marginally, e.g. southern Yemen — Watson 2002:16 fn., Ghaylḥabbān; that is Peninsular, not Mashriqi, territory). (4) SUN-LETTER (ʔidġām aš-šamsiyya) assimilation of the article ل to a following coronal is unchanged from Classical/MSA and inherited via the positional al-+coronal digraphs. NOT GROUP-WIDE — LEFT TO LEAVES (only documented here): (a) QĀF ق: the reflex is the classic Arabic sociophonetic variable and splits three ways INSIDE this group — glottal stop /ʔ/ in the big Mediterranean cities (Cairo, Jerusalem, Damascus, Beirut — Watson 2002:17), voiced /ɡ/ in Bedouin-type and Sudanic dialects (ar-SD, ar-TD, gilit ar-IQ) and /q/ maintained in the North-Mesopotamian qeltu dialects (Watson 2002:17, after Fischer & Jastrow 1980:52); learned/religious words keep /q/ even in ʔ-dialects (Watson 2002:17 ex.2: Cairene il-qurʔān, qarn, qarya). Watson 2002:9 makes the deeper point that the voiced reflex is a BEDOUIN feature, an axis that crosscuts geography — so no single group value is statable and the bare /q/ kept in the inherited grapheme table is the inheritance-root default, not a claim about the group. (b) ǦĪM ج: splits four ways inside the group (Watson 2002:16) — /dʒ/ in most Bedouin, rural Levantine and Mesopotamian dialects; /ɡ/ in Cairene; /ɟ/ in Upper Egypt and parts of Sudan (ar-SD's hallmark); /ʒ/ (no initial occlusive element) in the major Levantine cities. This node therefore does not override ج at all: the leaves own it, and the /dʒ/ the node passes through from its base is the Classical/MSA reading, not a group claim. (c) INTERDENTALS ث ذ ظ: the merger to stops [t d dˤ] is a SEDENTARY isogloss — Watson 2002:9 lists 'preservation of the Classical Arabic interdentals' among typical Bedouin features, and gilit Iraqi (ar-IQ) keeps θ/ð/ðˤ while Cairo/urban Levant merge. The stop-first candidate lists in the tables are the sedentary-koine inheritance default the urban leaves rely on (with the fricatives kept as second candidates for the retaining leaves); they are not a group-wide merger claim. ORTHOGRAPHIC READING BASE: graphemes/positional_graphemes inherit from the modern MSA leaf `ar` (not Classical `arb`), because the modern spoken varieties are written in MSA orthography — the onset-glide digraphs يَ/وَ (/ja/,/wa/, not diphthongs), the hamza carriers أ/إ as bare /ʔ/ before an explicit harakah, word-final ي/و preferring the long-vowel reading, and pausal tāʾ marbūṭa. Genealogy is unchanged (parent/ancestors still point at Classical arb); only the data-inheritance base moves. INPUT CONTRACT: fully diacritised (tashkeel) Arabic.

sources.author: Watson, J.C.E.
sources.title: The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic
sources.notes: Read (full PDF, printed folios verified against the running heads). p.9: the East/West split at the Salum–(Sudan–Chad border) line; the West reduces the /a i u/ triad to a two-vowel system and combines iambic stress with unstressed-vowel deletion (Moroccan smin vs Cairene simīn; bṛṛa vs baṛṛa), while the East retains short vowels and trochaic stress ('katab vs ka'tab); same page: the bedouin–urban axis crosscuts geography — voiced qāf and interdental preservation are Bedouin features, which is why neither is statable group-wide. p.16 (§2.1.6): the ǧīm reflex geography — /dʒ/ Bedouin/rural Levant/Mesopotamia, /ɡ/ Cairene and Taʿizz, /ɟ/ Upper Egypt/parts of Sudan, /j/ Gulf/Syrian desert, /ʒ/ Beirut–Damascus–Jerusalem. p.17 (§2.1.9): the qāf reflex geography — /q/ maintained in qeltu; /ʔ/ in the big Mediterranean cities; /ɡ/ in Bedouin dialects; /q/ kept in learned words even in ʔ-dialects (ex.2: il-qāhira, il-qurʔān, qarn, qawmi, qarya).

sources.author: Versteegh, K.
sources.title: The Arabic Language (2nd edition)
sources.notes: Not obtained to a readable page on this pass (2nd edition is paywalled); cited as reported for the standard handbook presentation of the Eastern/Western primary split of the modern dialects and of the Bedouin/sedentary axis (ch. 'The Dialects of Arabic'; the same typology Watson 2002:9 credits to Versteegh 1997:144 for the Bedouin feature list). The load-bearing page-verified citations on this node are Watson 2002:9, 16-17.

sources.author: Holes, C.
sources.title: Modern Arabic: Structures, Functions, and Varieties
sources.notes: Not obtained to a readable page on this pass; cited as reported (via Watson 2002:16-17, which cites Holes 1995:59-61 for the urban-Levantine /ʒ/ and glottal-stop qāf areas) for the sedentary/Bedouin reflex distributions summarised in the notes.

sources.author: Brustad, K.
sources.title: The Syntax of Spoken Arabic: A Comparative Study of Moroccan, Egyptian, Syrian, and Kuwaiti Dialects
sources.notes: Not obtained to a readable page on this pass; cited as reported for the comparative frame in which Moroccan patterns against the three Eastern dialects (Egyptian, Syrian, Kuwaiti) — a syntax study whose introductory typology matches the East/West split modelled here; no phonological rule on this node rests on it.

stress.notes: Arabic stress is QUANTITY-SENSITIVE: it falls on a syllable because that syllable is heavy, not because of any orthographic ending, so no ending table can express it. The cascade (Ryding, A Reference Grammar of MSA, CUP 2005, §2.3; Watson, The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic, OUP 2002, ch.3): a superheavy final syllable (CVːC/CVCC) takes the stress (kiˈtaːb); otherwise a heavy penult (CVː/CVC) does (muˈdarris); otherwise the antepenult (ˈmadrasa). max_onset=1 because Arabic onsets are obligatory and hold exactly one consonant — a medial cluster splits, closing the preceding syllable, and that boundary is what makes the penult of mudarris heavy. INPUT CONTRACT: weight is only recoverable from a complete transcription, so this requires diacritized input; an undiacritized skeleton has no vowels to weigh. The registered wikipron gold carries no stress marks, but the benchmark strips stress from both sides by default, so this costs no PER.
